nativeRange.habitat: Floodplain lakes, oxbows, and slow-moving riverine habitats over soft substrates; often in shallow, warm, slightly acidic blackwater or clearwater areas with leaf litter and driftwood

decorPreferences: leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves)
dense planting (Amazon sword, Java fern, Anubias)
smooth rounded pebbles or caves for shelter

diet.notes: The Rust Corydoras is a benthic forager that accepts a wide variety of sinking foods. Sinking pellets and wafers work well as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, Tubifex, and daphnia. This species also grazes on detritus and small organisms in the substrate. Feeding at lights-out gives the corydoras competition-free access to food. Relying on dry foods alone is generally not recommended; including varied frozen or live foods tends to improve condition and breeding readiness.

goodWith: dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma — with similar water parameters)
avoidWith: large catfish that may compete for bottom space
fish requiring hard alkaline water incompatible with Amazonian parameters

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Corydoras rabauti / Osteogaster rabauti. Verify image availability at FishBase summary page.

summary: A small, rust-orange-and-black armoured catfish from the upper Amazon basin, the Rust Corydoras is popular in community aquariums for its peaceful nature and diligent bottom-scavenging. Best kept in groups of six or more in soft, slightly acidic water. The trade name Corydoras rabauti remains common despite a 2024 reclassification to Osteogaster rabauti.
careNotes:
The Rust Corydoras (Corydoras rabauti) is a hardy, beginner-friendly armoured catfish from the upper Amazon, Solimões, and Rio Negro drainages of Brazil and Peru. It reaches approximately 5–6.5 cm standard length, with females typically slightly larger than males. The species is distinguished by its rusty-orange body coloration contrasting with a dark lateral band extending onto the caudal peduncle ventral surface — a feature useful for separating it from the similar C. zygatus.

A group of at least six individuals is strongly recommended, as this species is gregarious and shows more natural foraging and social behavior in larger numbers. A 90 × 30 cm footprint (approximately 80 L) is a practical minimum. Fine sand substrate at least 3 cm deep is important: these fish probe the substrate with sensitive barbels and abrasive substrate damages them, leading to bacterial infections. Maintain water soft and mildly acidic (pH 5.5–7.2, gH 1–15), reflecting Amazonian conditions; though the species adapts to neutral water in the trade, it thrives best in softer conditions.

The Rust Corydoras is a facultative air-breather, using a modified portion of intestine to extract oxygen from atmospheric air — it periodically darts to the surface. Ensure the surface is accessible and not blocked by thick plant mats. The pectoral-fin spines are mildly venomous and can cause a painful puncture wound when handling; use a net rather than bare hands when transferring fish.

A 2024 comprehensive phylogenomic revision by Dias et al. (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) resurrected the genus Osteogaster, reassigning this species as Osteogaster rabauti. The genus Corydoras remains valid for other species. Trade use of the name “Corydoras rabauti” is expected to continue for years; the scientific revision is widely noted but not yet universally adopted in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Moderately easy to breed with conditioning and a trigger. Feed a varied diet including live or frozen foods for several weeks before attempting. Use a 2:1 male-to-female ratio. Spawning is triggered by a partial (50–70%) cool water change with slightly cooler, well-oxygenated water, mimicking seasonal rainfall. Females carry 2–4 eggs clasped between pelvic fins and deposit them on tank glass, plant leaves, or spawning mops; a single spawning may produce up to 100 eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 24–26°C. Remove eggs or fry to a separate rearing tank to avoid predation. Fry initially require infusoria or commercial fry foods, transitioning to freshly hatched brine shrimp within the first week. Albino and long-fin variants are established in the trade.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras rabauti
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (55–65 mm SL), temperature (20–27°C), pH (5.5–7.2), hardness (18–215 ppm), native range (Rio Javarí; Brazil/Peru border, possibly Colombia), habitat (floodplain lakes, oxbows, riverine), diet (omnivore, sinking foods plus frozen/live), tank minimum (90 × 30 cm), grouping (4–6+), breeding trigger (cool water change), and venomous pectoral spine note.

additional.name: FishBase — Osteogaster rabauti
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5.0 cm SL), temperature (23–25°C), native range (upper Amazon, Solimões, Negro basins), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes, Osteogaster per 2024 revision), trophic level 3.0, demersal freshwater habitat. FishBase now uses Osteogaster rabauti reflecting the Dias et al. 2024 reclassification.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Osteogaster rabauti
additional.notes: Supplementary data: adult size 55–65 mm, countries (Brazil, Peru, Colombia), depth range 2–25 m, breeding details (2–4 eggs between pelvic fins, ~100 eggs per spawn, 3–4 day incubation), venomous pectoral spines, facultative air-breathing, and confirmation of 2024 Dias et al. reclassification to Osteogaster. First described 1941 by LaMonte.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoradinae revision changes names for hundreds of species
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing reporting on the Dias et al. 2024 Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society revision; confirms Corydoras paraphyly resolved into seven genera including Osteogaster, Hoplisoma, Brochis, Gastrodermus, Scleromystax, Aspidoras, and Corydoras sensu stricto.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General corydoras care guide used to cross-check trade husbandry consensus: minimum group size 6, smooth sand or gravel substrate, diet (sinking wafers, frozen bloodworms, live blackworms), temperature 22–27°C, pH 6.5–7.8, tank minimum 20 gal (75 L) for most species, easy breeding via cool water changes. No C. rabauti-specific page available.

fish.breedingNotes: Condition breeders with live or frozen foods over several weeks before attempting to spawn. Spawning is typically triggered by a partial (50–70%) water change using slightly cooler, well-oxygenated water. A ratio of two males to one female tends to produce good results. Females deposit 2–4 sticky eggs per clutch on tank glass or plant leaves, with a single spawning producing up to around 100 eggs in total. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days. Fry should first be offered infusoria or commercial fry foods, transitioning to freshly hatched brine shrimp within the first week. Albino and long-fin strains are established in the trade.
